lines.greek: ὅς τις ἐν ἀμφιάλῳ Ἰθάκῃ βασιλεύσει Ἀχαιῶν·
κτήματα δʼ αὐτὸς ἔχοις καὶ δώμασιν οἷσιν ἀνάσσοις.
μὴ γὰρ ὅ γʼ ἔλθοι ἀνὴρ ὅς τίς σʼ ἀέκοντα βίηφιν
κτήματʼ ἀπορραίσει, Ἰθάκης ἔτι ναιετοώσης.
ἀλλʼ ἐθέλω σε, φέριστε, περὶ ξείνοιο ἐρέσθαι,
ὁππόθεν οὗτος ἀνήρ, ποίης δʼ ἐξ εὔχεται εἶναι
γαίης, ποῦ δέ νύ οἱ γενεὴ καὶ πατρὶς ἄρουρα.
ἠέ τινʼ ἀγγελίην πατρὸς φέρει ἐρχομένοιο,
ἦ ἑὸν αὐτοῦ χρεῖος ἐελδόμενος τόδʼ ἱκάνει;
οἷον ἀναΐξας ἄφαρ οἴχεται, οὐδʼ ὑπέμεινε
γνώμεναι· οὐ μὲν γάρ τι κακῷ εἰς ὦπα ἐῴκει.
τὸν δʼ αὖ Τηλέμαχος πεπνυμένος ἀντίον ηὔδα·
Εὐρύμαχʼ, ἦ τοι νόστος ἀπώλετο πατρὸς ἐμοῖο·
οὔτʼ οὖν ἀγγελίῃ ἔτι πείθομαι, εἴ ποθεν ἔλθοι,
οὔτε θεοπροπίης ἐμπάζομαι, ἥν τινα μήτηρ
ἐς μέγαρον καλέσασα θεοπρόπον ἐξερέηται.
ξεῖνος δʼ οὗτος ἐμὸς πατρώιος ἐκ Τάφου ἐστίν,
Μέντης δʼ Ἀγχιάλοιο δαΐφρονος εὔχεται εἶναι
υἱός, ἀτὰρ Ταφίοισι φιληρέτμοισιν ἀνάσσει.
ὣς φάτο Τηλέμαχος, φρεσὶ δʼ ἀθανάτην θεὸν ἔγνω.

interlinear: whoever in sea-encircled Ithaca shall-be-king of-the-Achaeans;
but-your-possessions yourself may-you-keep, and over-your-own-house may-you-rule.
for-may-not that-man come, whoever you unwilling, by-force,
of-your-possessions shall-rob, while-Ithaca is-still inhabited.
but I-wish you, best-of-men, about the-stranger to-ask:
from-where is this man? of-what land does-he-claim to-be,
and-where is-his lineage and native field?
does-he-bring some news of-your-father coming,
or his own need desiring does-he-come-here?
how, having-sprung-up, at-once he-is-gone, nor did-he-wait
to-be-known; for-not at-all a-base-man in the-face he-resembled.'
and-him in-turn Telemachus, prudent, answering addressed:
'Eurymachus, truly indeed the-homecoming has-perished of-my father;
so neither in-news any-longer do-I-trust, if from-somewhere it-should-come,
nor of-prophecy do-I-take-heed, whatever-one my-mother,
into the-hall having-called a-soothsayer, may-inquire-of.
and-this stranger of-mine is-my-father's-guest-friend, from Taphos he-is,
and-Mentes, of-Anchialus wise-hearted, he-claims to-be
the-son, and over-the-Taphians, oar-loving, he-rules.'
so spoke Telemachus, but-in-his-mind the-immortal goddess he-knew.
literal: as to who shall be king of the Achaeans in sea-encircled Ithaca;
but may you keep your possessions and rule over your own house.
May no man ever come who by force, against your will,
shall rob you of your goods, while Ithaca is still inhabited.
But I wish, best of men, to ask you about the stranger:
where is this man from? Of what land does he claim to be,
and where is his lineage and his native field?
Does he bring some news of your father's coming,
or does he come here seeking some need of his own?
How he sprang up and was gone at once, nor waited
to be known; for in the face he seemed no base man.'
Then prudent Telemachus answered him:
'Eurymachus, truly my father's homecoming is lost;
so I trust no news any longer, wherever it may come from,
nor do I heed any prophecy that my mother may inquire of,
when she has called a soothsayer into the hall.
This stranger is my father's guest-friend, and he is from Taphos;
he claims to be Mentes, son of wise-hearted Anchialus,
and he rules over the oar-loving Taphians.'
So spoke Telemachus, but in his mind he knew the immortal goddess.
translations.elizabethan:
as touching which of us shall hold the crown
of the Achaeans in sea-encircled Ithaca.
Meanwhile keep thou thy goods, and rule thy house.
Let no man come to strip thee of thine own
by force against thy will, while Ithaca
has people in it. But I fain would ask,
good sir, about the stranger. Whence is he?
What land does he lay claim to? Where his line
and his own native fields? Does he bring word
of thy returning father? Or does he
come here upon some errand of his own?
How suddenly he sprang away, nor stayed
to make himself known to us. Yet his face
was not the face of any common man."
Then prudent Telemachus made answer:
"Eurymachus, my father's coming home
is lost. I trust no messages now, whence
soever they come; nor do I heed the seers
my mother calls into the hall to question.
This stranger is my father's guest-friend, come
from Taphos. Mentes he declares himself,
the son of Anchialus, the wise of heart,
who rules the Taphian men that love the oar."
So spoke Telemachus, but in his heart
he knew the deathless goddess.
translations.modernist:
about which of us is going to be king of the Achaeans in sea-encircled Ithaca. But keep your property and run your own house. I hope no man ever comes and takes your things away from you by force, while there are still people living in Ithaca.

But I want to ask you about the stranger, if you do not mind. Where is he from? What country does he say he comes from? Where is his family and his own land? Does he bring news that your father is coming? Or is he here on business of his own? He got up and went in a hurry. He did not wait around to be introduced. He did not look like a nobody, either."

Then Telemachus answered him. He had sense.

"Eurymachus. My father's homecoming is gone. I do not believe news any more, wherever it comes from. I pay no attention to prophecies, whatever my mother asks when she calls a seer into the hall.

That man is my father's guest-friend. He is from Taphos. He says he is Mentes, the son of Anchialus, a wise man, and that he rules the Taphians, who love the oar."

That is what Telemachus said. But in his mind he knew it had been the immortal goddess.
translations.storybook:
about which of us will be king of the Achaeans in sea-encircled Ithaca. But you must keep your own property and be master of your own house. I hope no man ever comes and takes your things away from you by force, as long as there are people living in Ithaca!

But I should very much like to ask you about that stranger. Where did he come from? What country does he say he belongs to? Where is his family, and his own land? Does he bring news that your father is coming home? Or did he come here about some business of his own? He jumped up and disappeared so quickly, and never waited to be properly introduced! And he certainly did not look like an ordinary man."

Then sensible young Telemachus answered him.

"Eurymachus, my father is never coming home. I do not believe any news any more, no matter where it comes from. And I take no notice of prophecies, whatever my mother asks when she calls a fortune-teller into the hall.

That stranger is an old friend of my father's, and he comes from Taphos. He says he is Mentes, the son of wise Anchialus, and that he is the chief of the Taphians, who love their oars."

That is what Telemachus said out loud. But in his heart, he knew perfectly well that his visitor had been an immortal goddess.

murray.text: But I am fain, good sir, to ask thee of the stranger, whence this man comes. Of what land does he declare himself to be? Where are his kinsmen and his native fields? Does he bring some tidings of thy father's coming, or came he hither in furtherance of some matter of his own?

How he started up, and was straightway gone! Nor did he wait to be known; and yet he seemed no base man to look upon.” Then wise Telemachus answered him: “Eurymachus, surely my father's home-coming is lost and gone. No longer do I put trust in tidings, whencesoever they may come,

nor reck I of any prophecy which my mother haply may learn of a seer, when she has called him to the hall. But this stranger is a friend of my father's house from Taphos. He declares that he is Mentes, son of wise Anchialus, and he is lord over the oar-loving Taphians.”

So spoke Telemachus, but in his heart he knew the immortal goddess. Now the wooers turned to the dance and to gladsome song, and made them merry, and waited till evening should come; and as they made merry dark evening came upon them. Then they went, each man to his house, to take their rest.
